> Same string ~218x over the 300 s window, docker ps showed bgp Up throughout.

This almost seems like it's not transient, if the same error message happens 218 times out of (I'm guessing) 300 times. Is this happening on a particular platform, or topology, or something?

> > Same string ~218x over the 300 s window, docker ps showed bgp Up throughout.
> 
> This almost seems like it's not transient, if the same error message happens 218 times out of (I'm guessing) 300 times. Is this happening on a particular platform, or topology, or something?

Hey @saiarcot895, referencing this testplan: https://elastictest.org/scheduler/testplan/6a2cb3212047c3c4a9f929d8?leftSideViewMode=detail&testcase=configlet%2Ftest_add_rack.py&type=console ,  it's specific to the kvm/t1-lag virtual PR pool, not on physical switches. The test body passed and only the config_reload teardown errored, and the retry on another kvm/t1-lag instance passed. Over the last 60 days that exact "Not all critical processes are healthy" signature shows up 200 times, all on kvm/t1-lag and zero on physical. 
In this run, 32 of 52 docker exec bgp probes hit the OCI rc=127 fork/exec race while 20 succeeded with bgpd/zebra RUNNING, and every sibling container returned rc=0 at the same instants with bgp Up, which is why it is transient and the fix would address the same

Automated CI triage (build [1135627](https://dev.azure.com/mssonic/be1b070f-be15-4154-aade-b1d3bfb17054/_build/results?buildId=1135627)):

The earlier `Pre_test Detect skip_expiry changes` Azure-agent `__pycache__` cleanup error is **resolved on this build** (Pre_test stage = succeeded). The three remaining Elastictest failures all show the same scheduler/infra signature, **not code regressions**:

- **`Test impacted-area-kvmtest-t0 by Elastictest`** ([job log](https://dev.azure.com/mssonic/be1b070f-be15-4154-aade-b1d3bfb17054/_build/results?buildId=1135627&view=logs&jobId=c4781836-bf04-5f60-aed4-f5b1830934f2)) — testplan `6a2a1947cf5a529eecf80d22` FAILED at 3438s with `errors: 3, failures: 0, skipped: 1374, passes: 863`. Single failing test: `lldp/test_lldp_syncd.py|||2` → `RUN_TEST_CASE_FAILED, stop whole test plan`. Six unrelated modules were `Test Module Cancelled: ... was revoked after running for N minutes (no pause/cancel operation recorded)` (pfcwd, bgp/test_bgp_update_timer, bgp/test_bgp_peer_shutdown, telemetry, ssh, static_route) — classic Elastictest scheduler revocation pattern.
- **`Test impacted-area-kvmtest-t1-lag by Elastictest`** ([job log](https://dev.azure.com/mssonic/be1b070f-be15-4154-aade-b1d3bfb17054/_build/results?buildId=1135627&view=logs&jobId=0b468b80-2747-5b0b-68cc-d9ebd25f9f5e)) — same signature: testplan `6a2a187ccf5a529eecf80d20` FAILED at 2655s. Single failing test: `lldp/test_lldp_syncd.py|||2`. Four unrelated modules revoked (sub_port_interfaces, everflow, vxlan_bfd_tsa, bgp_update_replication).
- **`Test impacted-area-kvmtest-t2 by Elastictest`** ([job log](https://dev.azure.com/mssonic/be1b070f-be15-4154-aade-b1d3bfb17054/_build/results?buildId=1135627&view=logs&jobId=3ab7146d-ea64-5d52-0097-33a9aae59fb6)) — testplan `6a2a195095ed954f0d3d78bb` FAILED at 4837s. Single failing test: `bgp/test_prefix_list_suppress.py|||2`. Seven modules revoked (reboot, ip/test_mgmt_ipv6_only, ecn_config_update, cacl_application, nbr_health, neighbor_mac_aging, sched_strict_priority).

This PR only modifies:
- `ansible/generate_topo.py` — `d32u32s2-mix` group layout (320→192 panel ports, 4-of-5 → 4-of-3 groups)
- `ansible/vars/topo_t0-isolated-d32u32s2-mix.yml` — VLAN/host-interface remap for the new layout
- `ansible/roles/test/vars/testcases.yml` — adds `t0-isolated-d32u32s2-mix` to `continuous_reboot`/`reboot` topology lists
- `tests/common/plugins/conditional_mark/tests_mark_conditions.yaml` — adds `t0-isolated-d32u32s2-mix` to one conditional-skip allowlist
- `tests/qos/qos_sai_base.py` — adds `t0-isolated-d32u32s2-mix` to `SUPPORTED_T0_TOPOS`

The failing `lldp/test_lldp_syncd.py` and `bgp/test_prefix_list_suppress.py` cases run on **standard `t0`, `t1-lag`, and `t2`** topologies, not on the new `t0-isolated-d32u32s2-mix` topology this PR adds — there is no path by which the diff can affect them. Classification: flaky/infra, requires a re-run.

(Note: a prior `/azp run` posted from this account on 2026-06-10 was rejected by azure-pipelines[bot] with `Commenter does not have sufficient privileges for PR 25245 in repo sonic-net/sonic-mgmt`, so this comment intentionally omits a retrigger — `@mssonicbld` already kicked off build 1135627 earlier today, and any further retrigger needs to come from a privileged commenter.)

Automated CI triage (branch state):

> ## Per-Commit Review
> ### Commit 1 — Fix neighbor-type routing and harden bring-up
> * `"sonic" in neighbor_type and neighbor_type != "csonic"` is fragile — substring matching with an exclusion list does not scale if more sonic-derived types appear. A helper like `is_sonic_ssh_neighbor()` or a set lookup would be clearer.
> * `vcisco` was added to `supported_vm_types` without explanation — is this intentional for this PR or an unrelated drive-by?
> * The interface readiness check uses `| first` on a Jinja2-filtered list. Ansible may evaluate the `command:` argument before the `when:` guard runs, causing a blow-up if the filtered list is empty.
> 
> ### Commit 2 — Add run_command/run_command_list to CsonicHost
> No issues. Clean and correct.
> 
> ### Commit 3 — Role-aware config template, host fetch, neighbor-type gating
> * The initial `configdb-csonic.j2` ships with `iface.get('lacp') is defined` which is always True (None is defined -> True in Jinja2). This breaks routed-interface rendering for T1/T2 until commit 8 fixes it — bad for `git bisect`.
> * Backplane is hardcoded as `Ethernet2` with lanes `29,30,31,32` — broken for multi-link topologies until commit 9. Again hurts bisectability.
> * `props.nhipv4` / `props.nhipv6` are used unconditionally in the BGP_NEIGHBOR section with no `| default()` guard. Topologies that do not define these will blow up with an undefined variable error.
> 
> ### Commit 4 — Fix first_found template lookup to search role templates dir
> * This commit proves commit 3 was merged broken. Should be squashed into commit 3 for a clean history.
> 
> ### Commit 5 — Document host team kernel module requirement
> No code issues. Good documentation of a non-obvious failure mode.
> 
> ### Commit 6 — Add out-of-tree team-module build + Secure Boot signing
> * The `curl` commands hardcode kernel source version `v6.8` — should note more prominently that users must match their actual kernel major.minor.
> * `sudo cp if_team.h /usr/src/linux-headers-\$K/include/linux/` modifies system-managed header files with no note about whether this survives kernel upgrades.
> 
> ### Commit 7 — Document automatic bonding 802.3ad PortChannel fallback
> No issues. Good UX improvement — users no longer need to deal with team module problems.
> 
> ### Commit 8 — Fix routed-interface rendering in configdb-csonic.j2
> * Critical fix (`is defined` -> `in iface`), but this patches a bug introduced in commit 3. Should be squashed into commit 3 so intermediate commits are not broken.
> 
> ### Commit 9 — Support multi-link (LAG) neighbors on T1-LAG/T2
> * In the `sonic_naming=True` code path, `SONIC_BP_TEMPLATE` may still use a static backplane name regardless of `num_fp_links`. Current `add_csonic.yml` passes `sonic_naming: false` which sidesteps this, but the code path is misleading if anyone enables sonic_naming later.
> * Lane formula (`(_bp_num - 1) * 4 + 25`) uses magic numbers with no constant or comment explaining why lanes start at 25. Fine for VS where lanes are synthetic, but worth a one-line comment.
> 
> ### General

Thanks for the thorough per-commit review — this was really helpful. I've pushed a force-update (rebased branch) that addresses everything below. Summary of what changed:

## Commit 1 — Fix neighbor-type routing and harden bring-up

- **Fragile substring + exclusion list** (`"sonic" in neighbor_type and neighbor_type != "csonic"`): removed the exclusion. I reordered the branches so the explicit `elif neighbor_type == "csonic"` is handled *before* the generic `elif "sonic" in neighbor_type` branch. I kept the substring (rather than an exact `== "sonic"`) on purpose: that branch intentionally routes the whole SSH-based SONiC family — both `sonic` and `vsonic` — to `SonicHost`, so an exact match would have broken `vsonic`. Added a comment explaining the ordering/intent.
- **`vcisco` in `supported_vm_types`**: good catch, that was an unrelated drive-by. Removed it — this PR only adds `csonic`.
- **`| first` on a Jinja2-filtered list in `command:`**: reworked. The filtered list is now computed once into a fact, and the readiness task uses a guarded index:
  ```yaml
  - name: Collect front panel interfaces expected from CONFIG_DB
    set_fact:
      csonic_fp_interfaces: "{{ configuration[hostname]['interfaces'].keys() | select('match', '^Ethernet[0-9]+$') | list }}"
  - name: Wait for front panel interfaces to be created from CONFIG_DB
    command: docker exec ... ip link show {{ csonic_fp_interfaces[0] }}
    when: csonic_fp_interfaces | length > 0
    ...
  ```
  Ansible evaluates `when` before templating the module args, so `[0]` is never rendered on an empty list.

## Commit 3 — Role-aware config template, host fetch, neighbor-type gating

- **`iface.get('lacp') is defined` always-True bug** and **hardcoded `Ethernet2` backplane**: agreed these hurt `git bisect`. See the history note below — both the routed-interface fix and the `first_found` fix are now squashed into this commit, so it is no longer broken in isolation. (The dynamic `Ethernet{K+1}` backplane is genuinely part of the multi-link feature, so that stays in the multi-link commit, which is where it's first claimed to be supported.)
- **Unguarded `props.nhipv4` / `props.nhipv6`**: fixed. Both exabgp next-hop entries are now emitted only when defined *and* non-empty, and both go through the same `joiner(",")` as the real BGP peers, so there's never a dangling/leading comma regardless of which (0/1/2) are present:
  ```jinja2
  {% if props.nhipv4 is defined and props.nhipv4 %}
  {{ bgp_comma() }}
          "{{ props.nhipv4 }}": { ... }
  {% endif %}
  {% if props.nhipv6 is defined and props.nhipv6 %}
  {{ bgp_comma() }}
          "{{ props.nhipv6 }}": { ... }
  {% endif %}
  ```

## Commit 4 — Fix first_found template lookup

Squashed into Commit 3, as requested — it was a fixup of the template introduced there.

## Commit 6 — Out-of-tree team-module build + Secure Boot signing

- **Hardcoded `v6.8` kernel source**: added a prominent callout above the build block that the `B=...linux/v<MAJOR>.<MINOR>` URL **must** match the running kernel's `uname -r` major.minor, and inlined the same warning on the `B=` line.
- **System-managed headers / kernel upgrades**: documented that copying the headers into `linux-headers-$(uname -r)` and dropping modules under `/lib/modules/$(uname -r)` does **not** survive a kernel upgrade — after a kernel bump the build (and signing) must be repeated for the new `uname -r`.

## Commit 8 — Fix routed-interface rendering (`is defined` → `in iface`)

Squashed into Commit 3 so the intermediate commits aren't broken.

## Commit 9 — Multi-link (LAG) support

- **`sonic_naming=True` static backplane name**: you're right that this path is misleading. The deployment always passes `sonic_naming: false` (see `add_csonic.yml`). I added a comment in `csonic_network.py` calling out that the `sonic_naming=True` branch is legacy/experimental and that its static `SONIC_BP_TEMPLATE` does not account for `num_fp_links`, so the backplane placement must be revisited before anyone enables it.
- **Lane magic numbers**: added a comment explaining the `(N-1)*4 + 25` lane base — these are synthetic VS lanes that just have to be unique and consistent with the SONiC-VM `lanemap.ini`; 25 is the first usable lane.

## General

- **`git bisect` / introduce-then-fix chain**: addressed by squashing the two fixup commits (first_found, routed-interface) into Commit 3. The branch is now 8 commits with no broken intermediate states; I verified the final tree is byte-identical to before the rewrite.
- **No unit tests for the template**: added `ansible/roles/sonic/templates/tests/test_configdb_csonic.py`. It renders `configdb-csonic.j2` with synthetic neighbor configs and asserts on exactly the logic flagged here — valid-JSON/comma handling across all nexthop combinations, backplane placement at `Ethernet{K+1}` for multi-link neighbors, the routed-vs-LAG-member split, and the lane math. It depends only on `jinja2`, so it runs standalone (`python3 .../test_configdb_csonic.py`) or under pytest. 9 cases, all passing.
- **`joiner(",")` JSON fragility**: noted — I've kept the joiner pattern to stay consistent with the existing sonic-mgmt templates, but the new unit test now guards against trailing/leading-comma regressions in the cSONiC template specifically.
- **PR size / splitting**: understood. Given the commits are now cleanly separated by concern (routing/bring-up, host, template, docs, multi-link, tests) and each builds green, I'd prefer to keep them in one PR for review continuity, but I'm happy to split into infra-fixes / multi-link / docs+tests if you'd still like that.

Thanks again!

Thanks @Copilot for the review. Addressed in 3500b9197: **Loader fix (HWSKU cross-platform collision)** The HWSKU shard layer was keyed solely by `` filename, so two `platforms/

/hwskus/.json` shards under different platform directories with the same filename silently overwrote each other based on directory-walk order. Fixed by nesting the merged tree as `hwskus..` and updating the resolver to look up by `(platform, hwsku)`. Added regression test `test_hwsku_same_name_under_different_platforms` exercising two platforms with the same HWSKU filename but different bodies (26/26 tests pass). **`firmware_overrides` slot (no producer yet)** Good catch — `DutInfoLoader` does not currently populate `active_firmware_version` in `BASE_ATTRIBUTES`, so the `firmware_overrides` resolver layer is silently inert today. This is by design: the slot is reserved in the schema so contributors do not invent ad-hoc keys for firmware-conditional overrides; the producer will land with the CDB FW upgrade test infrastructure. Added an inline comment in `_resolve_priority` documenting this so the next reader does not file the same question. **Stale wrapped-form examples in HLDs** All flagged JSON examples in `docs/testplan/transceiver/test_plan.md` (platform / HWSKU / vendor / per-PN) and `docs/testplan/transceiver/dom_test_plan.md` (4 per-PN) have been rewritten to the scope-rooted body form that matches the actual on-disk shard contract. Priority-hierarchy references to `hwskus.` updated to `hwskus.

.` for consistency with the new merge layout.

Thanks for the fix, @congh-nvidia! One concern with the approach: the added `pause: seconds: 3` is a fixed, unconditional sleep, which is racy — 3s can be too short on a slow/loaded DUT (chronyd still not ready, so `chronyc burst` fails again) and is wasted time when it's ready sooner, so it tends to reintroduce the same flakiness under load.

Could we gate on chronyd actually being ready instead of sleeping? The cleanest fix is to make the burst itself resilient — drop the new pause task and retry the burst until it succeeds:

```yaml
          - name: Force rapid NTP polling with chrony
            become: true
            command: chronyc burst 4/4
            register: chrony_burst_result
            until: chrony_burst_result.rc == 0
            retries: 5
            delay: 2
            changed_when: false
```

This polls the real readiness condition — it proceeds as soon as chronyd accepts the command and tolerates slow platforms without a magic constant. Happy to re-review once it polls for readiness rather than sleeping. Thanks!

Adding the entry is the documented way to onboard a new ASIC here (before it, an SPC6 DUT hard-fails with "Please add ASIC in the SUPPORTED_ASIC_TO_NEXTHOP_SELECTED_MAP"), and the risk is contained to `mellanox_spc6_hwskus`, so a wrong mapping would only affect SPC6 and is self-correcting.

One thing for the record, @ytzur1: `SUPPORTED_ASIC_TO_NEXTHOP_SELECTED_MAP` encodes empirically-observed flow->nexthop hashing — it exists to catch SAI hash-function changes across releases, and the per-flow assertion is skipped on the virtual switch (`if vendor == "vs": return`), so PR CI doesn't exercise this path. Mapping `spc6` to `spc_asic_flow_map` therefore assumes SPC6 hashes identically to SPC1-SPC5. That's a reasonable default (all five prior Spectrum generations share that map), but it's a hardware claim CI can't confirm. Could you confirm it was verified on real SPC6 hardware, and add the observed flow->nexthop result from that run to the PR description? It won't make the squash/changelog, but it'll live on the PR as the reference baseline if SPC6's hash ever diverges.

Thanks @davidm-arista . It actually already uses wait_until under the hood, wait_monit_mem_last_ok(timeout) is wait_until(timeout, 1, 0, self.is_monit_mem_last_ok), polling every 1s and returning as soon as monit reports OK. The 200 isn't a sleep; it's just the upper bound passed to wait_until, so a healthy run still completes in a few seconds and only a genuine failure ever approaches it.
 
 I picked 200 to match the existing detect-side call in this same file (wait_monit_mem_last_failed(200) in consumes_memory_and_checks_container_restart()); the module default MONIT_MEMORY_CHECK_TIMEOUT is even larger at 700. The old 30s was the outlier and was too tight for slow ARM/Marvell-Prestera (Nokia-7215), where monit's recover cycle legitimately exceeds 30s and produced a ~48% flake. Happy to add an inline comment clarifying that this is a poll ceiling if that helps readers.

@XuChen-MSFT are there any tests that exercise routeCheck directly (or rely on routeCheck results) that could be affected by this change? Is there a chance that we would skip a valid failure because of this?

Following up on @anders-nexthop's question above — I looked into the routeCheck paths so we can settle common-ignore vs. per-test. Short version: this doesn't appear to hide a valid failure, and the tests that depend on routeCheck aren't affected by it.

**Does anything rely on routeCheck results that this could weaken?** A few fixtures/tests do exercise routeCheck, but they all assert on it programmatically, not through LogAnalyzer: `temporarily_disable_route_check` in `tests/conftest.py` runs `sudo route_check.py` and asserts `rc == 0` (`wait_for_route_check_to_pass`); `start_route_checker_on_duthost` / `stop_route_checker_on_duthost` in `tests/common/fixtures/duthost_utils.py` assert on the monit service status from `get_monit_services_status()`; and `tests/vxlan/test_vnet_bgp_route_precedence.py` asserts `route_check.py` passes via `_check_route_check_pass`. LogAnalyzer only scans syslog for unexpected ERR patterns at teardown — it can't suppress a test's own assertion on a command's exit code or on `monit summary`. So none of these are weakened by adding the ignore.

**Could we skip a valid failure?** A persistent routeCheck failure is still caught independently of LogAnalyzer by the `check_monit` sanity check (`tests/common/plugins/sanity_check/checks.py`), which reads `monit summary` and fails when the `routeCheck` Program service is not `Status ok`. And the detailed `route_check.py` ERR line is already on the common ignore list (the line directly above this one); since route_check.py logs to syslog by default (`--log_to_syslog` defaults to True, so the monit-invoked run still logs), the `monit 'routeCheck' status failed` line always co-occurs with an already-ignored `route_check.py` ERR. This PR is effectively ignoring the wrapper for a detail that's already ignored — net-new LogAnalyzer blindness is ~zero, while the sanity-check path still catches a real, persistent failure.

On @ZhaohuiS's point about scoping per-test rather than globally: that would be more surgical, but since the underlying `route_check.py` ERR is already globally ignored, a per-test ignore here wouldn't actually add protection against masking a real route inconsistency — the signal LogAnalyzer could catch is already suppressed upstream. If we ever want LogAnalyzer (not just the monit sanity check) to catch persistent route mismatches, the line to revisit is the existing `route_check.py` global ignore, which is out of scope for this PR.

Net, the common-ignore looks safe here. Happy to go per-test instead if preferred, but it wouldn't add protection given the above.

The intended flow is: send DSCP 3 → classified to block queue 2 on egress → trimmed (block queue starved) → trimmed copies land on trim queue 6 (egress blocked + small buffer) → overflow → TRIM_DRP_PKTS. The `TRIM_QUEUE_PROFILE` constant changed from `"egress_lossy_profile"` (a real profile) to `"trim_queue_test_profile"` with `{"pool": "egress_lossy_pool", "dynamic_th": "-7", "size": "0"}`. With `size=0, dynamic_th=-7`, the trim-queue buffer is intentionally starved — works for the counter test by design, but the other tests expect trimmed copies to actually be received, and they depend on enough shared-pool headroom remaining for the trim queue. A pointer to a CI run or a "ran locally on SN6, all pass" line in the PR thread would settle this.

The 30-second `wait_until(check_trim_drop_counter_zero)` after re-enabling egress (and the helper itself) was removed. The deleted comment said it was needed because TRIM_DRP_PKTS is a derived counter (TRIM_PKTS − TRIM_TX_PKTS) that drains gradually after the queue is unblocked, and the next test step needed a zero baseline. What changed to make this wait unnecessary? A line in the commit message or PR body would close the loop.

4. `"nvidia_sn6" in platform.lower()` — substring match catches the whole SN6 family (sn6010, sn6500, …). If intentional, a brief comment confirming that's the gate (rather than specific SKUs) would help.

**Issue 1 — Multi-ASIC: `k == "database"` filter misses per-ASIC database containers**

```python
database_containers = {k: v for k, v in containers_in_namespaces.items() if k == "database"}
```

On multi-ASIC DUTs, per-ASIC database containers are named `database0`, `database1`, etc. These do not match `k == "database"` and fall into `non_database_containers`, so they are killed in Phase 1 while SSH is still expected to be stable. If killing `database0` causes the same SSH hang (same root cause), the original bug reproduces on multi-ASIC platforms.

---

**Issue 2 — Comment describes per-namespace check that is not implemented**

The inline comment says:
> _"On multi-ASIC, also check per-namespace config files (e.g. redis0/sonic-db/database_config.json)"_

The code only checks `/var/run/redis/sonic-db/database_config.json`. The per-namespace check is not present. The comment is either describing unimplemented work or is misleading.

**Substantive items:**

| # | Item | Resolution |
|---|------|-----------|
| 1 | `KVM_PLATFORM` -> `is_vs_device` broadening | Intentional -- `is_vs_device` correctly covers all VS-backed images where PDU is unavailable and SysRq reboot is needed. Kept as-is. |

The `except Exception` is kept because `stop_critical_processes` can fail with various error types depending on timing (`RunAnsibleModuleFail`, SSH errors, etc.). Logging at warning level with the exception object for traceability. Removed redundant `str()`. |
| 3 | Database alerting coverage gap | Added docstring note that database alerting is intentionally not validated. Filed as a known limitation rather than a follow-up since verifying database alerts requires a fundamentally different approach (in-process syslog read before SSH dies). |
| 4 | `wait_time` double duty | Fixed -- introduced separate `db_config_timeout` (120s default, 600s for modular chassis) independent of the boot `wait_time`. |
| 5 | Why not back-port | Will back-port to 202511. |

**Nits -- all fixed:**
- Trailing whitespace removed
- Em-dash changed to `--`
- Redundant `str()` removed
- Import ordering cleaned up

**Also fixed a bug found during KVM testing:** The recovery fixture was calling `ensure_all_critical_processes_running` *before* `database_config.json` was ready, causing `docker exec database supervisorctl start redis` to fail with `unix:///var/run/supervisor.sock no such file`. Reordered to: wait for db config -> ensure processes -> check interfaces.

**Top-level BGP confederation block — should this be removed?**

Looking at the converged BGP block in `lt2-o128-tor.j2` and `t1-64-lag-tor.j2`, the `{% set ns = namespace(...) %}` section emits this just after `vrf MGMT / rd 1:1 / exit`:

```
router bgp <conv_asn>
 vrf MGMT
  rd 1:1
 exit
 bgp confederation identifier <ns.confed_asn>      # ← top-level (default VRF)
 bgp confederation peers <ns.confed_peers ...>
!
 vrf VRF_A
  ...
  bgp confederation identifier <vrf_bgp.confed_asn>  # ← per-VRF
  bgp confederation peers ...
```

A couple of concerns I'd like to double-confirm with you:

1. The top-level lines apply to the **default VRF**, but in the converged template the default VRF only declares `router bgp <asn>` plus an empty `vrf MGMT` — there are no BGP peers at the default-VRF level (all sessions live under `vrf <name>`). EOS BGP confederation is per-VRF and is not inherited from the default VRF into user VRFs, so this top-level block looks like it has no functional effect on the user-VRF sessions.

2. The data is also slightly inconsistent with the per-VRF block: the top-level `confed_peers` is the de-duplicated union of every VRF's `confed_peers` (so it ends up containing the local ASN of each VRF), while each per-VRF `confed_peers` correctly excludes its own ASN.

3. For comparison, the other converged templates don't do this:
   - `t0-isolated-d2u254s1-tor.j2` / `t0-leaf.j2` / `t0-v6-leaf.j2` (added in #22171): no confederation at all.
   - `t1-lag-spine.j2` (modified in this PR, commit "Keep only the functional per-VRF BGP confederation block addition"): per-VRF only.

   Only `lt2-o128-tor.j2` and `t1-64-lag-tor.j2` have the extra top-level block.

Would you mind confirming whether the top-level block is intentional? If it's just leftover from an earlier iteration, I'd suggest removing it so all three converged templates handle confederation the same way (per-VRF only). If it's intentional, could you add a comment explaining what it's meant to achieve in the default-VRF context?

**`announce_routes.py:fib_lt2_routes` — two small follow-ups**

1. `multi_vrf_data['convergence_mapping']` is accessed without `.get()`:

   ```python
   multi_vrf_data = topo.get('convergence_data', {})
   if multi_vrf_data:
       all_vms = sorted([vrf for vrfs in multi_vrf_data['convergence_mapping'].values() for vrf in vrfs])
   ```

   The earlier commit message ("use .get('convergence_mapping', {}) to avoid KeyError") suggests this was meant to be defensive, but the code still uses direct subscript. If a future topology file has `convergence_data` without `convergence_mapping`, this raises `KeyError`. Suggest:
   ```python
   mapping = multi_vrf_data.get('convergence_mapping', {})
   all_vms = sorted([vrf for vrfs in mapping.values() for vrf in vrfs])
   ```

2. `T0_ASN_OFFSET = 200` shares the leaf/tor ASN space with T1/UT2. The earlier commit message ("add assert that T0_ASN_OFFSET > len(t1_vms) + len(ut2_vms)") suggests an assertion was planned, but it's not in the final diff. Could you add it back? Otherwise once T1+UT2 grows past 200 the T0 ASNs will silently collide with the T1/UT2 ASNs.

@arista-nwolfe, Thanks for testing and for the detailed investigation!

The failure is consistently reproducible in our lab — crm_stats_ipv4_neighbor_used does not increment and crm_stats_ipv4_neighbor_available does not decrement after adding an out-of-subnet neighbor, despite the kernel accepting the entry and orchagent successfully programming it into SAI.

After discussing offline, it appears the CRM counter behavior for out-of-subnet neighbors varies across different platform and SAI versions. The exact same symptom was not reproduced on other testbeds, though a related CRM counter inconsistency (used increments but available does not decrement) has been observed and is currently being tracked.

The fix extends the existing host-IP-add pattern to broadcom-dnx platforms — ensuring the neighbor address falls within the interface's configured subnet before adding it. This approach is safe for all platforms: on implementations where CRM already handles out-of-subnet neighbors correctly, adding the host IP has no impact on CRM accounting.

@yaopingz Thanks for the fix — adding the `sleep(1)` does address the immediate symptom (the loop was effectively a busy spin and returned `False` almost instantly), and I'm fine with this going in as a 202511 hot-fix. However, `wait_for_http` has a few more issues that this PR does not address, and I'd like to suggest a follow-up:

```python
def wait_for_http(host_ip, http_port, timeout=10):
    started = False
    tries = 0
    s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)   # created ONCE
    s.settimeout(2)
    while not started and tries < timeout:
        try:
            s.connect((host_ip, http_port))                 # same socket reused
            started = True
        except socket.error:
            tries += 1
            time.sleep(1)
    return started
```

1. **Socket is created once and reused after a failed `connect()`.** POSIX leaves the socket state unspecified after a failed `connect()`; conforming code must `close()` and create a new socket before retrying. In practice on Linux you tend to get `EALREADY` / `EISCONN` / `EINVAL` on subsequent attempts, so once the first `connect()` fails, every retry is doomed. With the new `sleep(1)`, the function now politely takes the full `timeout` seconds before still returning `False`, which means the caller blocks for up to 60 s on the BGP-scale path and then proceeds anyway.
2. **`timeout` is misleadingly named** — it is really a max-retry count. With `settimeout(2)` per attempt plus `sleep(1)`, the wall-clock for `timeout=60` ranges from ~60 s (connection refused, fast) to ~180 s (filtered / unreachable, slow). It should be a monotonic deadline so the parameter matches its name.
3. **`s` is never closed**, so we leak a file descriptor on every call that exits this path during scale tests.
4. **The underlying `socket.error` is swallowed silently**, which is exactly why this regression was hard to diagnose in the first place.

Suggested follow-up that fixes all four in one pass:

```python
def wait_for_http(host_ip, http_port, timeout=10):
    deadline = time.monotonic() + timeout
    last_err = None
    while time.monotonic() < deadline:
        try:
            with socket.create_connection((host_ip, http_port), timeout=2):
                return True
        except OSError as e:
            last_err = e
            time.sleep(1)
    logging.warning("wait_for_http(%s:%s) timed out after %ss: %s",
                    host_ip, http_port, timeout, last_err)
    return False
```

`socket.create_connection` gives a fresh socket per attempt, applies the connect timeout cleanly, and the `with` block closes it. The deadline-based loop makes `timeout` an honest wall-clock budget.

Non-blocking — please consider a follow-up PR.

> Thanks for the Redfish suite — overall design is solid (session-scoped mTLS fixture, both off→on transitions checked for `PowerCycle`, negative cert tests). A few blocking issues in `tests/redfish/conftest.py` that I'd like resolved before merge.
> 
> ### Blocking
> **1. Password leaks to `ps` and shell-injection risk in `_bmc_ssh` / `_bmc_scp` (`conftest.py:108-129`)**
> 
> ```python
> ssh_cmd = "sshpass -p {pass_} ssh ... {cmd}".format(pass_=bmc_pass, ..., cmd=cmd)
> subprocess.run(ssh_cmd, shell=True, check=True, ...)
> ```
> 
> `sshpass -p <pw>` puts the cleartext BMC password in the process argv (visible to any `ps` on the sonic-mgmt host) for the lifetime of every SSH/SCP call. `bmc_pass` is also interpolated unquoted into a `shell=True` string — works today only because `creds.yml` happens to be shell-safe; will break or execute injected shell the moment the password contains `$`, `` ` ``, `'`, `"`, space, `;`, etc. `_bmc_scp` has the same pattern.
> 
> Suggested fix: use `sshpass -e` with `env={"SSHPASS": bmc_pass}`, drop `shell=True`, pass argv as a list.
> 
> **2. `_bmc_ssh` contract: docstring promises `(stdout, stderr, returncode)` but `check=True` raises (`conftest.py:106-123`)**
> 
> ```python
> def _bmc_ssh(...):
>     """... Returns (stdout, stderr, returncode)."""
>     result = subprocess.run(ssh_cmd, shell=True, check=True, ...)
> ```
> 
> With `check=True`, any non-zero exit raises `CalledProcessError` instead of returning the documented `returncode`. The only call site that wraps this in `try/except` is `_bmcweb_running`; every other caller (including `bmc_exec` used by tests) will surface an opaque traceback instead of a clean assertion. Either drop `check=True` and let callers inspect `rc`, or update the docstring + all call sites to expect raising.
> 
> **3. Inconsistent quoting between `bmc_exec` and direct `_bmc_ssh` callers (`conftest.py:82` and e.g. `:152`)**
> 
> ```python
> # bmc_exec wraps:
> def _exec(cmd):
>     return _bmc_ssh(..., "'{}'".format(cmd))
> 
> # _bmcweb_running also wraps:
> _bmc_ssh(..., "'docker exec {} supervisorctl status bmcweb'".format(BMCWEB_CONTAINER))
> ```
> 
> `_bmc_ssh` interpolates `cmd` raw into the ssh command line, so the implicit convention is "caller must pre-quote." That convention isn't enforced, isn't documented, and breaks the first time any caller does `bmc_exec("docker exec redfish bash -c 'rm /tmp/x'")` — `bmc_exec`'s outer `'...'` wrapper collides with the embedded `'` and the remote shell mis-parses. Please consolidate: have `_bmc_ssh` use argv-list `subprocess.run([..., "ssh", ..., cmd])` with `shell=False` so callers never quote.
> 
> **4. `bmc_creds` reads a hardcoded relative path and can silently return `password=None` (`conftest.py:34-49`)**
> 
> ```python
> creds_file = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__),
>                           "../../ansible/group_vars/all/creds.yml")
> ...
> return {"user": creds_data.get("sonic_login"),
>         "password": default_passwords[0] if default_passwords else None}
> ```
> 
> Three concerns: (a) the relative path only works in a sonic-mgmt checkout with that exact layout — containerized or downstream consumers will hit `FileNotFoundError` at fixture collection time; (b) if `sonic_default_passwords` is empty, `password=None` flows into `sshpass -p None ...` and surfaces as a confusing auth failure deep in a test, not a clear setup error; (c) using SONiC switch credentials (`sonic_login` / `sonic_default_passwords`) for the BMC assumes they match — most BMC infra has dedicated BMC creds. Please switch to `pytest_addoption` (`--bmc-username` / `--bmc-password`) or BMC-specific inventory vars, and `pytest_require(creds["password"], "BMC password not configured")` to fail loudly.
> 
> ### Please clarify
> **`bmc_ip` fixture: docstring says `<dut>-bmc`, code reads `duts[0]` (`conftest.py:25-31`)**
> 
> ```python
> """Return the BMC mgmt IP from the <dut>-bmc inventory entry."""
> dut_name = tbinfo["duts"][0]
> return duthosts[dut_name].host.options["inventory_manager"]
>     .get_host(dut_name).get_vars()["ansible_host"]
> ```
> 
> The code never appends `-bmc` — it just returns `ansible_host` of `tbinfo["duts"][0]`. In the BMC testbed entries I'm familiar with, `duts[0]` is the SONiC switch and the BMC is a separate `<switch>-bmc` inventory host, so this would aim Redfish/SSH at the switch mgmt IP. Could you confirm which is intended for `bmc-dual-mgmt` / `bmc-shared-mgmt`? If the docstring is right, the code likely needs `dut_name + "-bmc"`.
> 
> Non-blocking nits (banner-strip hardcoded to `Debian GNU/Linux 13`, `EXPECTED_MEMBERS={"bmc","bios","switch"}` blocks adding platforms, `test_reset_invalid_type` only checks `"error" in body` instead of asserting a `RedfishError` shape, no finalizer fixture so a failing `test_reset_power_cycle` leaves the x86 CPU in reset) — happy to file separately if useful, but the four above are the merge-blockers.
> 
> Reviewed by AI agent on behalf of Ying.

Thanks @yxieca for the review. Following the BMC HLD (#22688), which models the BMC as a DUT (`SonicHost`) and uses `duthost.shell()` in its examples, I moved the SSH/credential layer onto the `SonicHost` framework — `duthost.shell()` for commands and `duthost.copy()` for file transfer (the standard framework equivalent of `scp`) — removing `_bmc_ssh`, `_bmc_scp`, and `bmc_creds` entirely. That resolves the four blocking items at the root:

- **1. Password in `ps` / injection** — no `sshpass`, no `shell=True`; auth is over Ansible with inventory creds, so the password never hits a command line.
- **2. `check=True` vs. contract** — `duthost.shell(..., module_ignore_errors=True)` returns `rc` without raising, so `bmc_exec` honestly returns `(stdout, stderr, rc)`; setup steps fail loudly, teardown is best-effort.
- **3. Inconsistent quoting** — commands go to the remote shell as a single arg, so no caller pre-quotes (the `'{}'` wrapper is gone).
- **4. `bmc_creds`** — deleted; the BMC authenticates from its own inventory entry (`ansible_user`/`ansible_ssh_pass`), so no relative path, no silent `None` (`pyrequire(is_bmc())` gates non-BMC DUTs), and BMC-specific creds. This is the inventory-vars option you suggested.

**`bmc_ip` clarification:** in these topologies the `dut` entry *is* the `<switch>-bmc` host (the host-side switch is the separate `bmc_host`), so `duts[0]` is the BMC and the code was correct — I reworded the docstring and switched to `duthost.mgmt_ip`.

**Nits:** banner strip is gone with `_bmc_ssh`; `test_reset_invalid_type` now asserts the `RedfishError` shape (`code`+`message`), which also fixed the CodeQL `error_body` finding; added an autouse finalizer that restores the CPU to `On` after each reset test. On `EXPECTED_MEMBERS` — it's a required-*subset* check (`EXPECTED_MEMBERS - member_ids`), so it doesn't block adding platforms.

- **Route aggregation behavior:** Changing overwrite to append may introduce duplicates or unintended semantics for topologies other than BGP_SCALE_T1S. Confirm scope or add deduplication/clear rationale.
- **RX quiescence wait must be bounded:** Ensure `wait_for_rx_quiescence()` has a hard max timeout and cannot hang indefinitely under continuous traffic; document or expose thresholds.
- **Timeout handling visibility:** Nanomsg timeout handling must not silently mask failures—ensure logging + retry/threshold/clear failure signal so tests don’t pass without traffic.
- Add comments/docstrings explaining quiescence algorithm and ECMP/shared-route rationale.
- Extract poll interval/stability thresholds as constants.

Reviewed by AI agent on behalf of Ying.

@yxieca
 Thanks for the review. Addressing each point:

  1. Route append duplicates  — The append is safe for all topologies. The BGP_SCALE_T1S loop only runs when  "topo_name in BGP_SCALE_T1S" . For all other topologies, topo_routes[k] is initialized as {}, so `.get(IPV4, [])` returns [] and the append is equivalent to =. No duplicate risk.

  2. Max timeout on wait_for_rx_quiescence — covered by yejianquan — will add a 'max_timeout=120'  parameter. If counters don't stabilize within 120s, the loop breaks with a warning log and proceeds with current counters.

  3. Silently mask failures — Failures are not silently masked. '_send_with_retry' returns 'False'  on exhaustion, the caller tracks it in 'retry_failures', and logs the total at thread exit: "%d packets are sent (%d retry failures)".

  4. Add docstrings — 'wait_for_rx_quiescence' already has a docstring explaining the nn_agent pipeline drain and why polling replaces fixed sleep. The ECMP/route-append rationale is in the commit message.

  5. Extract constants — These are already named function default parameters (`stable_secs=10`, `poll_interval=1`), which serve the same purpose. Extracting to module-level constants would add indirection without improving readability.

  6. Log drain duration — 'calculate_downtime' already logs all rx/tx counters, totals, missing packet count, pps, and duration. Adding more logging in the quiescence function would be redundant.

  7. Over-broad exception handling  — The 'except Exception' with re-raise is intentional. ptf_dataplane.send() raises nnpy.NNError on timeout, but test_ipv6_bgp_scale.py doesn't import nnpy ( only available on the PTF host?). The guard if "timed out" not in str(e).lower(): raise ensures non-timeout exceptions propagate immediately. The only other caller
 of dataplane.send() in sonic-mgmt (test_xon_xoff.py) uses no error handling at all — we added retry handling specifically because the scale test (512 ports, 1024 exaBGP processes) triggers nn_agent CPU starvation that smaller tests never hit.
